properties.name: fig-03-01
properties.caption: Data quality pipeline routing. Semantic reasoning tasks route to LLM-assisted methods with dual-model verification; statistical tasks route to traditional methods with distributional checks. The design discipline is matching each sub-task to the right tool.
properties.description: Data quality pipeline routing diagram for Chapter 3

properties.name: fig-04-01
properties.caption: Extraction pipeline architecture. Raw text enters at left. Semantic chunking preserves meaning before extraction begins. Dual-model extraction runs independently; disagreements route to human review. Agreed extractions proceed through entity resolution with escalating ambiguity handling. Every extraction carries provenance to its source passage before reaching validated output.
properties.description: Extraction pipeline architecture diagram for Chapter 4

properties.name: confidence laundering
properties.summary: Pipeline design failure mode: converting uncertain, unverified extraction output into a structured artifact whose format implies verification that never occurred. Introduced in Ch 3, defined in Ch 4. Cross-project term — candidate for promotion to shared vocabulary.

properties.name: confabulation graph
properties.summary: A knowledge graph produced by an extraction pipeline that cannot trace its edges and nodes back to specific source passages with provenance. The graph looks structured but contains a mixture of extracted facts and model-generated inferences that cannot be distinguished.

properties.name: fig-01-01
properties.description: y
properties.caption: z

properties.name: fig-05-01
properties.description: Parallel consensus topology — Ch 5
properties.caption: Parallel consensus topology. Input fans out to N independently prompted models. Agreed outputs proceed through the decision rule with a versioned provenance record. Disagreements route to human review and rejoin the flow. The disagreement rate is your primary quality signal.

properties.name: fig-05-02
properties.description: Generator-critic loop topology — Ch 5
properties.caption: Generator-critic loop topology. Model A generates; Model B evaluates. Failed outputs return to the generator with specific rejection reasons. Four conditions terminate the loop: all checks pass, confidence threshold met, maximum iterations reached, or escalation to human review.

properties.name: fig-05-03
properties.description: LLM-as-Judge topology — Ch 5
properties.caption: LLM-as-Judge topology. A dedicated judge model evaluates generator output against a versioned rubric. Outputs scoring above threshold are accepted with provenance; below-threshold outputs route to human review.

properties.name: fig-05-04
properties.description: Topology comparison table — Ch 5
properties.caption: Topology comparison. Three multi-model topologies compared across task type, evaluation method, cost profile, primary failure mode, and use conditions.

properties.name: fig-06-01
properties.description: Parallel batch processing architecture — Ch 6
properties.caption: Parallel batch processing architecture. The rate limiter governs throughput against provider constraints; exponential backoff prevents cascading failures on rejection. N independent workers process batches with no shared context. The checkpoint store enables resume-on-failure without restart.

properties.name: fig-06-02
properties.description: Model abstraction layer — Ch 6
properties.caption: Model abstraction layer. Pipeline logic calls through a configuration-driven model interface that knows nothing about the specific model underneath. When the model changes, only the configuration changes.

properties.name: fig-06-03
properties.description: Pike's rules pipeline mapping table — Ch 6
properties.caption: Pike's five rules mapped to LLM pipeline design. Each rule surfaces a different class of engineering mistake: unverified assumptions about bottlenecks, premature optimization, failure to design for scale, hidden complexity, and schema-first design.

properties.name: fig-07-01
properties.description: Error classification decision table — Ch 7
properties.caption: Error classification and pipeline response. The key design decision is that the pipeline must classify each error type and respond differently. Retrying permanent errors wastes resources. Stopping on data errors prevents completion. Ignoring transient errors produces incomplete results.

properties.name: fig-07-02
properties.description: Transaction-safe checkpoint write pattern — Ch 7
properties.caption: Transaction-safe checkpoint write pattern. Writing to a temp file and atomically renaming prevents checkpoint corruption on crash. A crash during the write phase leaves the old checkpoint intact.

properties.name: fig-07-03
properties.description: Config validation flow — Ch 7
properties.caption: Config validation flow before full pipeline launch. Each check gates the next. A failed model name, unaccepted parameter, or failed smoke test stops the run before it processes 50,000 records on bad configuration.

properties.name: tbl-07-01
properties.description: Error classification table: three error types, pipeline responses, investigation priorities
properties.caption: Error classification and pipeline response. The key design decision is that the pipeline must classify each error type and respond differently.

properties.name: tbl-05-04
properties.description: Topology comparison table: parallel consensus, generator-critic loop, and LLM-as-judge across seven criteria
properties.caption: Topology comparison. Three multi-model topologies compared across task type, evaluation method, cost profile, primary failure mode, and use conditions. The right topology depends on task structure, cost constraints, and what signal you are optimizing for.

properties.core_argument: LLM-powered research workflows are stochastic systems that require an architectural design discipline distinct from both traditional software engineering and classical statistical methods; the choice of whether and how to use LLMs should be governed by evidence infrastructure, not just output quality.

properties.claims: LLMs cannot self-correct reasoning without external feedback, making iterative self-refinement loops a trap when evaluator and generator share the same model | A single-model pipeline buries uncertainty in confident-sounding output; the stochastic failure mode is coherent, fluent wrong answers that pass casual inspection | The self-refinement degradation pattern at inference time is structurally analogous to model collapse at training time, with recursive self-consumption in both cases | Perceived AI productivity gains are systematically overestimated relative to measured outcomes, with the METR RCT showing 19% slowdown and the DORA report showing measurable delivery degradation | Moving to higher levels of AI automation requires redesigning workflow architecture, not just adopting a better tool

properties.terminology_defined: stochastic liability: a property of the system you design around, not a defect you fix | stochastic tax: the overhead you accept every time you route a task through a probabilistic language model when a deterministic method would suffice | SFV (State Fidelity Validity): framework for ensuring AI-assisted pipelines preserve accuracy and integrity of accumulated internal state | levels of AI automation: five-level framework for AI workflow complexity from spicy autocomplete to specification-driven pipelines
properties.forward_promises: chapter-02: classification workflows applying design principles in action | chapter-05: ensemble patterns generalizing the single-model problem | chapter-08: evaluation harness infrastructure | chapter-09: SFV framework deep treatment | chapter-10: provenance engineering
properties.open_threads: Formal mathematical treatment of variance compounding across pipeline stages | Benchmarking methodology for research-grade workflows vs software development workflows | Economic models for calculating when AI pipeline investment pays off

properties.core_argument: Classification is the natural entry point for LLMs in statistical work, and reliability requires applying inter-rater reliability methodology to LLM raters; the dual-model cross-validation pattern borrowed from statistical survey methods transforms stochastic liabilities into quantifiable quality signals.
properties.claims: Dual-model cross-validation produces meaningful agreement signals when models are architecturally distinct and independently trained | Disagreement between independently trained models is signal about genuine taxonomic ambiguity, not noise to be suppressed | Preserving dual-modal assignments rather than forcing arbitration is a deliberate engineering choice when taxonomy ambiguity is real | Cohen's kappa applied to LLM rater pairs is a valid reliability measure by direct analogy to inter-rater reliability in human coding studies | The  total inference cost for classifying 6,954 items versus months of analyst time demonstrates the cost-effectiveness of the dual-model approach
properties.terminology_defined: dual-modal assignment: when two models both score high-confidence but assign different primary topics, both assignments are preserved rather than arbitrated | algorithmic echo chamber: when a single model's systematic biases dominate output, producing coherent and consistent but predictably skewed results | position bias: the order in which classification options are presented to a model affects its output, analogous to primacy and recency effects in survey methodology | ABBA design: counterbalancing technique that randomizes presentation order to detect and control for position bias
properties.forward_promises: chapter-05: dual-model pattern generalized into three multi-model topologies | chapter-08: golden questions evaluation infrastructure | chapter-09: model transience as formal SFV concern | chapter-10: provenance engineering for classification pipelines | chapter-14: full cost accounting framework
properties.open_threads: Formal independence analysis of training corpus overlap between commercial LLM vendors | Optimal arbitration threshold calibration methodology | Statistical power requirements for golden question test sets

properties.core_argument: Data wrangling tasks present a qualitatively different LLM design challenge than classification because the input itself is unreliable; the design discipline is routing each sub-task to the right tool, with LLMs handling semantic reasoning and traditional statistical methods handling distributional properties.
properties.claims: LLM-based imputation of missing values violates distributional properties that traditional methods like MICE preserve, making traditional methods superior for statistical estimation tasks | Fine-tuning is not cost-effective unless projected API spend exceeds approximately ,000 per year and workload is stable enough to absorb MLOps overhead | When input is noisy, dual-model disagreement becomes more valuable as a quality signal, not less, because single-model confidence is even less reliable | Applying LLMs to continuous variable correction fails because language models cannot enforce ratio constraints and distributional consistency | Traditional NLP methods are not inferior for tasks requiring steady, reproducible, predictable behavior
properties.terminology_defined: confidence laundering: converting uncertain unverified extraction output into structured artifacts whose format implies verification that never occurred (previewed here, defined fully in Ch 4) | semantic reasoning tasks: tasks where LLMs handle ambiguous natural language interpretation | statistical tasks: tasks requiring distributional properties and numerical consistency where traditional methods are superior | institutional deployment tax: the organizational overhead of finding budget, navigating security review, procuring hardware, setup, and quality benchmarking
properties.forward_promises: chapter-04: confidence laundering gets full treatment | chapter-12: security controls for data access | chapter-13: governance that prevents delivery | chapter-14: fine-tuning cost accounting framework
properties.open_threads: SLM capability trajectory for statistical coding tasks | Cost-effectiveness threshold for fine-tuning on NAICS/SOC tasks at different agency scales | Validation methodology for LLM-proposed schema mappings

properties.core_argument: Extraction creates new structured data from unstructured input, making it the most generative and therefore most dangerous domain workflow for verification; confidence laundering—where structured output format implies verification that never occurred—is the central failure mode, and every extraction design element exists to prevent it.
properties.claims: A knowledge graph that cannot trace every edge to a specific source text passage is not a knowledge graph but a confabulation graph | Structural confidence (typed nodes, labeled edges) launders stochastic uncertainty without removing it from the underlying data | Semantic chunking at natural boundaries is a prerequisite for reliable extraction; mechanical chunking destroys meaning before extraction begins | Multi-model extraction is not optional—it is the mechanism for getting reliability when ground truth is unavailable | The metadata-only input pattern reduces both security risk and confabulation risk by limiting the model's exposure surface
properties.terminology_defined: confidence laundering: when a pipeline converts uncertain extraction output into structured artifacts whose format implies verification that never occurred | confabulation graph: a graph that mixes extracted and inferred content without provenance distinguishing them | SFV Compression Fidelity (CF): sub-dimension measuring whether chunk boundary placement destroys semantic meaning | SFV Terminological Consistency (TC): sub-dimension measuring whether entity mentions are consistently resolved across sources | SFV State Provenance (SP): sub-dimension measuring whether every output element traces to a specific source passage | entity resolution escalation: tiered approach from exact string matching through embedding similarity to LLM disambiguation to human review
properties.forward_promises: chapter-05: ensemble extraction as multi-model pattern | chapter-09: SFV framework full treatment | chapter-10: provenance requirements for extraction artifacts | chapter-12: metadata-only input pattern for security
properties.open_threads: Automated provenance citation methods that force LLMs to output source spans | Formal taxonomy of extraction confidence levels | Entity resolution at scale across multi-source federal data systems

properties.core_argument: Multi-model design is the correct baseline for any LLM pipeline that claims reliability; single-model pipelines need justification because they hide uncertainty rather than measuring it, and the design discipline is choosing the right topology—parallel consensus, generator-critic loop, or LLM-as-judge—based on task structure and cost constraints.
properties.claims: A single-model pipeline cannot distinguish confident-and-correct from confident-and-wrong, making disagreement between models the primary quality signal in any multi-model design | Cross-model self-refinement requires different models for generator and critic; same-model self-refinement degrades through bias amplification, reward hacking, and convergence ceilings | LLM-as-judge exhibits systematic preference leakage when judge and generator are related models | Model selection diversity must target different failure modes, not just different architectures | When two models score closely on benchmarks, using both is better engineering than running more experiments to find the winner
properties.terminology_defined: parallel consensus topology: N models perform same task independently; agreement is quality signal; disagreement routes to escalation | generator-critic loop topology: generator produces output, independent critic evaluates and returns feedback for revision, with explicit termination conditions | LLM-as-judge topology: dedicated judge model evaluates generator output against a versioned rubric as a quality gate | preference leakage: systematic bias in judge models that favor outputs from related or same-family models | self-consistency: sampling multiple independent reasoning paths and selecting the most consistent answer
properties.forward_promises: chapter-06: how topology pieces execute physically (parallel vs serial, rate limits) | chapter-08: evaluation harness that validates topology choices | chapter-09: model selection drift as SFV concern
properties.open_threads: Formal cost modeling for composed multi-topology pipelines | Independence guarantees for models sharing training corpus subsets | Optimal topology selection criteria for statistical production environments

properties.core_argument: LLM API calls are the bottleneck in any pipeline, and the design discipline is maximizing useful work within the constraints of rate limits, latency, and context windows; force multipliers amplify good and bad design equally, so architectural discipline must precede scaling.
properties.claims: Parallelizing a serial task produces incorrect results; serializing a parallel task wastes time; task independence analysis is prerequisite to architecture selection | Exponential backoff with jitter is required infrastructure for any LLM pipeline that must survive rate limiting | Worker count is a two-constraint optimization bounded by available compute and provider rate limits | Model selection based on benchmark comparison consumes more engineering time than the performance difference is worth at production scale | The model is a replaceable component; architecture is the durable investment; configuration-driven model selection enables continuous cost optimization
properties.terminology_defined: embarrassingly parallel: tasks where each item can be processed independently without knowledge of other results | exponential backoff: retry strategy with progressively longer wait intervals (1s, 2s, 4s) to avoid cascading failures on rate limits | config-driven model selection: pipeline calls models through an abstraction layer so swapping models is a configuration change not a code rewrite | bottleneck node: the component that constrains pipeline throughput, nearly always the LLM API call
properties.forward_promises: chapter-07: full checkpoint and recovery architecture for long-running batch jobs | chapter-11: infrastructure reinvention problem and reusable module patterns | chapter-14: cost accounting framework for batch economics
properties.open_threads: Dynamic rate limit adaptation during pipeline execution | Optimal checkpoint granularity as a function of per-record cost | Multi-provider rate limit pooling strategies

properties.core_argument: Pipeline survivability requires config-driven architecture, progressive test infrastructure, and checkpoint-and-recovery as integrated design disciplines, not afterthoughts; the recursive nature of the problem—that the tools used to build pipelines are themselves stochastic—means the same discipline applies to the development process.
properties.claims: Config-driven architecture that externalizes all configurable parameters prevents both runtime failures and development-time drift from AI coding assistants | Model parameters have a shelf life; a temperature parameter removed between model versions invalidates configurations that assumed its presence | 75% of frontier AI coding models break previously working features during long-term codebase maintenance | Transaction-safe checkpoint writes (write temp, then atomically rename) prevent checkpoint corruption on crash | An append-only results file independent of the checkpoint provides recovery when the checkpoint itself fails | Error type determines response: transient errors retry, permanent errors escalate, data errors log-and-continue
properties.terminology_defined: transient error: API rate limit, network timeout, temporary server error that resolves without intervention | permanent error: invalid API key, deprecated model, removed endpoint that will never succeed on retry | data error: malformed input, unparseable response, schema mismatch that is a quality signal not an infrastructure failure | idempotent operation: running it twice produces same result as once, enabling safe retry | smoke test: quick sanity check run against a small batch before committing to full production run | regression test: automated re-run of test suite after any change to catch regressions
properties.forward_promises: chapter-09: why survivable pipelines can still produce invalid results (drift and SFV) | chapter-11: full treatment of recursive stochasticity in development toolchain
properties.open_threads: Formal specification of checkpoint granularity as optimization problem | AI coding assistant regression testing protocol | Checkpoint format portability across pipeline versions

properties.core_argument: Evaluation is architecture, not afterthought: the system that produces data must simultaneously produce the evidence that the data is trustworthy, and since no standard benchmarks exist for statistical production tasks, practitioners must build their own evaluation instruments using existing quality frameworks as scaffolding.
properties.claims: AI agents achieving 2.5% client-acceptable quality on real work while scoring near-expert on isolated benchmarks demonstrates that benchmark performance is not predictive of production pipeline reliability | Valid and reliable is a binary gate, not one dimension among many; if a system confabulates, no other trustworthiness property can be meaningfully evaluated | Multi-model agreement rate is itself an evaluation metric, not just a reliability measure—it provides evidence of construct validity when independently trained models converge | Continuous evaluation is an operating cost not a capital expenditure; evaluation is never complete because models update, requirements change, and edge cases emerge | The FCSM 20-04 and NIST AI RMF address the same concerns with different vocabulary; agencies should extend existing statistical quality practices rather than build parallel AI evaluation processes
properties.terminology_defined: golden test set: curated set of inputs with known-correct outputs used for regression testing across model updates and configuration changes | valid and reliable as gate: the foundational trustworthiness characteristic that all other AI trustworthiness assessments depend on | tailored crosswalk: translation of FCSM statistical quality dimensions to NIST AI RMF trustworthiness characteristics for a specific pipeline type | evaluation economics: the total cost of evaluation including inference costs for multi-model runs, human review time, and ongoing regression testing
properties.forward_promises: chapter-09: drift detection as continuous evaluation concern | chapter-10: provenance tracking as evaluation infrastructure
properties.open_threads: Confabulation detection specifications for specific statistical domains | Formal validation study methodology for the FCSM/NIST crosswalk | Statistical power requirements for golden test sets at different confidence levels

properties.core_argument: State Fidelity Validity is the author's original framework for understanding why LLM pipelines can recover perfectly from every failure and still produce invalid results; it reframes state degradation as a validity failure, not a reliability failure, because the instrument changes during use through five distinct threat mechanisms.
properties.claims: State degradation in LLM pipelines is a validity failure, not a reliability failure—a pipeline can reliably produce the same corrupted provenance | The five SFV threats (semantic drift, false state injection, compression distortion, state supersession failure, state discontinuity) collectively explain why config-unchanged pipelines produce changed outputs | Deterministic retrieval eliminates an entire class of state variability that embedding-based retrieval introduces | The model training cutoff is a massive permanent instance of State Supersession Failure (T4), making config-driven architecture the primary countermeasure | SFV is a precondition for all other validity claims in stateful AI pipelines; if operative history is corrupted, construct, internal, external, and statistical conclusion validity are all indefensible
properties.terminology_defined: State Fidelity Validity (SFV): the degree to which an AI-assisted pipeline preserves accuracy and integrity of accumulated internal state such that inferences at step N remain warranted by actual history of steps 1 through N-1 | T1 Semantic Drift: terminology mutates across turns without explicit redefinition | T2 False State Injection: system confabulates memory of decisions never established | T3 Compression Distortion: context window management silently strips nuance or alters meaning | T4 State Supersession Failure: outdated information persists despite explicit correction | T5 State Discontinuity: session boundary loss drops accumulated context | confabulation: generation of content presented as factual but not grounded in actual pipeline history (NIST AI 600-1 term)
properties.forward_promises: chapter-10: infrastructure required to implement SFV countermeasures
properties.open_threads: Formal measurement theory integration of SFV with Total Survey Error framework | Quantitative thresholds for acceptable SFV threat exposure by pipeline risk level | Automated SFV threat detection tooling

properties.core_argument: Provenance infrastructure is not overhead but the engineering mechanism that makes AI-assisted research defensible, and the eight requirements for research provenance—typed artifacts, explicit relationships, state machines, staleness propagation, state serialization, shared vocabulary, change gating, and automated verification—define what any provenance system must satisfy regardless of implementation.
properties.claims: Git satisfies none of the eight research provenance requirements because it tracks file-level diffs but not artifact semantics, relationships, state machines, or staleness propagation | AI coding partners change the cost equation for provenance infrastructure by absorbing the bookkeeping that killed adoption of systems like IBM DOORS | The dual-layer architecture (append-only event log plus graph database projection) provides both an immutable audit trail and queryable provenance without sacrificing either | Configuration control through specification documents creates an append-only decision record that makes the complete sequence of design decisions reconstructable | A corrupted checkpoint costs only the delta between results file and checkpoint when the results file is maintained independently of checkpoints
properties.terminology_defined: typed artifact: a tracked entity with semantic type (PaperSection, Result, Script, DataFile) that determines what states it can be in and what relationships it can have | staleness propagation: when an upstream artifact changes, all downstream dependents are automatically flagged as potentially stale through graph traversal | state machine: enforced lifecycle transitions for artifacts (proposed, draft, verified, published, stale) that prevent citing unvalidated artifacts as authoritative | shared vocabulary with write protection: canonical term definitions in a master database with read-only replicas in consuming projects | configuration control: discipline of gating changes to tracked artifacts through specification documents written before changes are made | graph-of-artifacts pattern: dual-layer architecture combining append-only event log (source of truth) and graph database (queryable projection)
properties.forward_promises: chapter-11: orchestration layer as coordination for provenance-tracked pipeline components
properties.open_threads: Automated specification document generation from design conversations | Cross-agency shared vocabulary federation patterns | Provenance infrastructure cost benchmarking for federal statistical workflows

properties.core_argument: The orchestration tool that statistical researchers actually need does not exist as a product because the market builds for software engineering, not for research provenance and statistical production; practitioners must evaluate tools against four dimensions—coupling, friction, transparency, and volatility—and bet on patterns rather than products.
properties.claims: No existing orchestration framework satisfies the research-grade provenance requirements from Chapter 10 because the market optimizes for deployment and monitoring of production services, not reproducibility and audit | Tight framework coupling means that when the framework changes—which happens frequently in AI tooling—the pipeline breaks at the framework layer rather than the model layer | 75% of frontier AI coding agents break previously working features during long-term codebase maintenance, applying SWE-CI evidence to the development toolchain | Context windows are working memory not long-term memory; critical state that lives only in the context window can be silently dropped during compaction | The development toolchain is part of the workflow architecture; recursive stochasticity means the same design discipline required for production pipelines applies to AI-assisted development
properties.terminology_defined: coupling: how tightly the orchestration layer binds to specific models, providers, or frameworks | friction: operational overhead including setup time, maintenance burden, debugging difficulty, and learning curve | transparency: ability to audit the sequence of operations performed and reproduce a run | volatility: likelihood the tool exists in current form in two years and migration cost if it does not | 90/10 rule: first 90% of capability from any tool is straightforward; last 10% (edge cases, compliance, integration) consumes 90% of effort | last mile: gap between works in development environment and works in production with actual constraints | recursive stochasticity: the development tools used to build LLM pipelines are themselves stochastic
properties.forward_promises: chapter-12: security and supply chain implications of orchestration dependencies | chapter-13: institutional deployment constraints on orchestration choices
properties.open_threads: Research-grade orchestration framework specification | MCP protocol adoption trajectory in federal statistical agencies | Context window management strategies for long-running multi-session research workflows

properties.core_argument: Security in AI workflows is a system property that enables rather than restricts work, and the Five Safes framework—already used by international statistical agencies—provides a natural organizing principle for extending existing data governance practices to cover the specific considerations LLM workflows introduce.
properties.claims: A local model making network calls for tokenizer configuration on first load is not offline, demonstrating that assumptions about system containment require empirical verification not assertion | Consumer-tier AI services typically reserve the right to use inputs for model improvement while enterprise and government tiers do not, making tier identification a prerequisite for safe data determination | LLMs can memorize and reproduce training data fragments, requiring AI-generated outputs to undergo the same statistical disclosure control review as any other output regardless of production method | Model provenance (who trained it, on what data, under what governance) is a trust decision that cuts across safe settings, safe data, and safe outputs | Security is not a gate passed once; it is present in every design decision from model selection to data handling to output review
properties.terminology_defined: Five Safes framework: safe projects, safe people, safe settings, safe data, safe outputs—a framework for providing data access for legitimate purposes while managing risk | safe project: whether this specific AI use justifies its controls | safe people: whether users are trained to work with AI appropriately and IT staff understand AI workflows | safe settings: whether the technical environment is secure, authorized, and contained | safe data: whether appropriate protection has been applied before data enters the AI workflow | safe outputs: whether results are non-disclosive and have undergone appropriate review | model provenance: who trained the model, on what data, under what corporate governance and jurisdiction | supply chain: the full dependency tree including tokenizers, embedding models, evaluation frameworks, Python packages, and container images
properties.forward_promises: chapter-13: organizational reality of getting security-approved AI workflows into production
properties.open_threads: Automated pipeline network call auditing for offline containment verification | Government-specific model card standards for Five Safes compliance documentation | Disclosure risk assessment methodology for LLM-generated text outputs

properties.core_argument: The gap between a working pipeline and a deployed production system is organizational, not technical; governance that prevents delivery is a governance failure, and the compressed tenets—particularly governance must enable execution and deliver capability quickly—are the standards against which every institutional friction point should be measured.
properties.claims: FedRAMP authorization timelines create a structural temporal mismatch with the AI tool landscape that cannot be resolved by working harder or writing better justifications | Authorizing platforms and patterns rather than specific model versions is the single highest-leverage change in government AI procurement | Organizations that implement tiered governance with dual metrics measuring both protection and enablement report faster authorization timelines without increased incident rates | Governance-as-gate creates a binary failure mode: teams stop trying or use tools without authorization, both of which violate the compressed tenets | Distributed AI capability across the workforce has more organizational impact than concentrated AI capability in a centralized unit
properties.terminology_defined: institutional deployment tax: multi-month overhead of security review, procurement, infrastructure setup, and quality benchmarking required before a working pipeline processes its first production record | visible capability gap: the political leverage created when one team using modern tools demonstrably outperforms another team using legacy approaches | ATO (Authority to Operate): authorization process designed for stable 5-10 year systems that AI workflows structurally violate through continuous model updates | tiered governance: risk-based control levels where low-risk uses get light-touch approval and high-risk production pipelines get full review | governance inversion: shifting from governance-as-gate to governance-as-infrastructure where technical controls enforce boundaries in real time
properties.forward_promises: chapter-14: cost accounting that makes economic case for organizational investment
properties.open_threads: Formal model for measuring governance effectiveness as both protection and enablement | Institutional change management methodology for transitioning from gate to infrastructure governance | Cross-agency authorization framework for shared AI infrastructure

properties.core_argument: Every cost discussion about AI pipelines is meaningless without the counterfactual cost of the status quo alternative; the design discipline this book teaches is itself a cost-reduction strategy, because designed pipelines use cheaper models, fewer retries, and less human review than undesigned ones doing the same work.
properties.claims: The  inference cost for 6,954 survey questions versus ,200 manual equivalent demonstrates that API inference cost is the smallest cost category in any honest comparison | Development cost is a one-time investment while manual process cost is recurring, making pipeline cost advantage compound over multiple production cycles | Inference costs represent only the smallest cost category; development costs dominate initial investment and operational costs are the chronically underestimated long tail | Evaluation-driven model selection—using the cheapest model that passes the golden test set quality gate—is cost optimization as architecture rather than one-time decision | Batch statistical workflows are naturally cost-advantaged for AI processing because they have no latency requirements, predictable volume, and retry tolerance
properties.terminology_defined: cost taxonomy: four categories (inference, development, operational, opportunity) that must all be counted for an honest AI cost comparison | evaluation-driven model selection: using the cheapest model that passes the quality gate for a specific task rather than brand-driven model selection | opportunity cost: cost of work not done while people perform repetitive tasks that AI pipelines could handle | batch economics: cost characteristics of batch processing versus interactive use, including off-peak scheduling and retry tolerance | designed vs undesigned pipeline: same task with and without right-sized model, batching, caching, optimized prompts, and evaluation infrastructure
properties.open_threads: Standardized cost accounting methodology for federal agency AI procurement decisions | Dynamic cost optimization as model pricing changes | Total cost of ownership modeling for statistical agency AI programs

properties.name: ch01 bottleneck shift and new citations

properties.claims: The $15 inference cost for 6,954 survey questions versus $23,200 manual equivalent demonstrates that API inference cost is the smallest cost category in any honest comparison | Development cost is a one-time investment while manual process cost is recurring, making pipeline cost advantage compound over multiple production cycles | Inference costs represent only the smallest cost category; development costs dominate initial investment and operational costs are the chronically underestimated long tail | Evaluation-driven model selection—using the cheapest model that passes the golden test set quality gate—is cost optimization as architecture rather than one-time decision | Batch statistical workflows are naturally cost-advantaged for AI processing because they have no latency requirements, predictable volume, and retry tolerance
